us-politics.headline: Trump Threatens Iran With Strikes Over Strait of Hormuz
us-politics.body:
On Easter Sunday, Trump posted a profanity-laced threat to bomb Iran's power plants and bridges if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened, and declined to rule out ground troops if no deal is reached. The threats came as US forces continue operations against Iran following the conflict's escalation.

Reactions split sharply. House Republicans largely backed the pressure campaign, with one arguing any ground deployment should target enriched uranium specifically. Democrats pushed back hard - the top Armed Services Democrat cited cost as a primary concern, while another argued Iran's grip on the strait poses a greater strategic threat than its nuclear program.

A former Trump adviser warned the aggressive rhetoric makes a negotiated deal less likely. Marjorie Taylor Greene broke with Trump, calling the Easter threat 'insane.' NATO tensions are also rising, with Spain's airspace restrictions signaling fractures in alliance unity.

sources.title: House Republican says he doesn’t think ground troops will be needed to open the Strait of Hormuz
Marjorie Taylor Greene rails against ‘insane’ Trump after Easter Iran threat
Top Armed Services Democrat says Iran war ‘mistake in large part because of the cost’
Trump declines to rule out ground troops if Iran doesn’t make a deal
Trump threatens Iran with ‘Hell’ over Strait of Hormuz in profane post
Trump roils NATO as pressure builds over Strait of Hormuz
House Republican: ‘Only purpose’ for US boots on the ground in Iran is ‘to get the enriched uranium’
Former Trump adviser on president’s latest Iran threat: ‘Not going to get a deal’
House Democrat: Iran’s control of Strait of Hormuz ‘more strategically vital’ than nuclear weapon
Trump drops Easter Sunday f-bomb in new threat to Iran
Trump vows US will strike Iran’s power plants, bridges if Strait of Hormuz is not reopened
The ‘post-global’ NATO: Spain’s airspace ban signals the death of universal alliances

us-politics.headline: Trump Fires Bondi and Noem, Denies Further Cabinet Shake-Up
us-politics.body:
Trump dismissed Attorney General Pam Bondi and Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, then told reporters no further Cabinet changes are planned. The firings came without public explanation and drew immediate scrutiny over the DHS vacancy.

Senate Democrat Tim Kaine said Bondi's dismissal should serve as a warning to whoever takes the job next, suggesting the role carries political risk under this administration. NPR framed the firings alongside what it described as Trump moving to walk back campaign promises. House Minority Leader Jeffries separately called on Speaker Johnson to end the House recess given a lapse in DHS funding.

sources.title: Trump says to not expect Cabinet shake-up after Bondi, Noem firings
Jeffries calls on Johnson to end House recess amid DHS funding lapse
Kaine: Bondi firing ‘should be a lesson’ for next attorney general nominee
Politics chat: Trump fires Attorney General Bondi, seeks to break campaign promises

us-politics.headline: Democrats Eye 2028, Debate How to Win Back Voters
us-politics.body:
Arizona Senator Ruben Gallego said he is actively considering a 2028 presidential run, offering one of the clearest signals yet of who may challenge for the Democratic nomination. Rep. Ro Khanna argued the party must engage with unconventional media figures like streamer Hasan Piker, warning that Democrats who avoid such platforms will lose future elections.

A separate opinion piece cautioned that Democratic messaging on affordability risks backfiring if the party cannot deliver tangible results before the midterms. Maryland Governor Wes Moore added his voice to the economic debate, arguing that ending foreign wars is the single most effective lever for lowering gas prices.

sources.title: Moore: Ending foreign wars ‘best thing that we could do’ to lower gas prices
Khanna: Those saying ‘don’t engage’ with personalities like Hasan Piker ‘will cost us future elections’
Gallego on possible 2028 presidential run: ‘We have to look at it’
‘Affordability’ could eventually bite Democrats in the bum

us-politics.headline: Pope Leo Calls for Peace; NASA Defends Trump Budget Cuts
us-politics.body:
Pope Leo used his first Easter address to deliver a message of peace, speaking directly to a world marked by active conflicts including the US-Iran war. The address drew wide attention given its timing and the new pontiff's commanding tone.

On the domestic front, NASA's administrator defended the administration's proposed budget cuts ahead of the 2027 fiscal year, arguing the agency can maintain core missions including lunar exploration. Critics have raised concerns that reductions could undermine the Artemis program and cede ground to China in the race back to the moon.

sources.title: NASA administrator defends Trump’s proposed budget cuts
On Easter, Pope Leo delivers commanding message of peace to a world at war
The White House vows America will never give up the moon again

au-politics.headline: Human Rights Commission Probes NSW Racial Discrimination Claim
au-politics.body:
The Australian Human Rights Commission is investigating an allegation of racial discrimination against the NSW government, in a case that raises questions about how state agencies treat Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people. Details of the specific complaint remain limited, but the investigation signals formal scrutiny of government conduct at the highest oversight level.

Separately, a family court has ordered the return of an Aboriginal child moved 1,700km from their remote Northern Territory community, ruling the relocation was not in the child's best interests. The case highlights ongoing tension between child protection systems and the rights of Indigenous families and communities to keep children connected to country and culture.

sources.title: Australian Human Rights Commission investigates alleged racial discrimination by NSW government
Aboriginal child moved 1,700km from remote NT community should be returned, family court rules

au-politics.headline: Youth Detention Cells Condemned as Harmful, Watchdog Demands Upgrade
au-politics.body:
Australia's justice watchdog has called for urgent upgrades to youth detention cells it describes as harmful, warning that current conditions actively obstruct access to justice for young people. The findings put pressure on state governments to fund infrastructure improvements in a sector that has faced sustained criticism over the treatment of juvenile detainees.

The call comes amid broader scrutiny of how Australia's legal system handles vulnerable young people, particularly Indigenous youth who are significantly overrepresented in detention. Advocates argue that poor physical conditions compound existing systemic failures rather than rehabilitate.

sources.title: Watchdog seeks upgrade to 'harmful' youth cells to improve access to justice

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Drives Up Used EV Prices as Servo Owners Struggle
au-politics.body:
Australia's ongoing fuel price crunch is reshaping the secondhand car market, with used electric vehicle prices rising as more buyers look to exit combustion engines. Dealers and buyers cite the sustained cost of petrol as the primary driver, suggesting the crisis is accelerating the EV transition faster than policy alone has managed.

At the same time, petrol station owners are warning the price squeeze is making their businesses unviable. Some operators are openly questioning whether running a servo is worth it, pointing to thin margins and customers venting frustration at the bowser. The two trends together paint a market under real structural pressure.

sources.title: Price crunch has some servo owners asking: 'Is it really worth doing this?'
‘Doesn’t make sense to hold onto a combustion engine’: used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits

au-politics.headline: Swift Parrot Habitat Logged After Repeated Call Recordings
au-politics.body:
A patch of Tasmanian forest where the critically endangered swift parrot was recorded calling dozens of times has been logged, raising sharp questions about whether environmental protections are working. The swift parrot is one of Australia's most at-risk birds, and conservationists argue the logging demonstrates that documented wildlife presence is not sufficient to trigger protection under current rules.

The case is likely to intensify debate ahead of any federal election over the adequacy of Australia's environmental laws, particularly the long-delayed reforms to the Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act. Critics say the gap between recording a threatened species and protecting its habitat remains dangerously wide.

sources.title: The swift parrot’s distinctive call was recorded dozens of times in a patch of Tasmanian forest. Then the forest was logged

world.headline: US-Iran War Escalates as Rescue Mission Sparks New Threats
world.body:
Trump declared victory after the rescue of a US airman from a remote part of Iran, but analysts warn the operation has deepened the conflict rather than resolved it. Iranian missiles struck a residential building in Haifa and an industrial area in southern Israel, while Israel continued strikes on Iranian targets. Iran's internet shutdown has now broken records for duration, cutting off millions of civilians.

Trump followed the rescue with an expletive-filled social media post threatening Iran, and warned of 'hell' if Iran does not reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Political scientist John Mearsheimer sees no signs of a quick end to the conflict. Iran's sports minister separately said the country's World Cup participation may depend on relocating matches away from the United States.

sources.title: Analysis: Trump declares victory in Iran war after rescue, but threats to US operation still loom
How rescue of US airman in remote part of Iran unfolded
Trump threatens Iran in expletive-filled social media post
Frustration grows as Iran’s wartime internet shutdown breaks grim record
Video captures Iranian missile striking residential building in Haifa
Iranian missile strikes industrial area in southern Israel
Trump threatens ‘hell’ for Iran over Hormuz Strait as deadline approaches
Mearsheimer: No signs of quick end to US-Israel war on Iran
Iran’s World Cup participation depends on relocation from US, minister says
Why an attack on Bushehr nuclear plant would be catastrophic for the Gulf

world.headline: Gaza Bombardment Continues as Hamas Rejects Disarmament Demands
world.body:
Hamas's armed wing flatly rejected Israeli and US demands for disarmament, calling them unacceptable as a condition for any ceasefire deal. Israeli air strikes hit a crowd in Gaza City, adding to a mounting civilian death toll. Conditions in displacement camps have deteriorated sharply, with rodent infestations now spreading through tent settlements.

Christians in Gaza marked Easter under bombardment, with many refusing to observe traditional celebrations. Israel's far-right minister Itamar Ben-Gvir outlined plans to introduce a 'death row' system for Palestinian prisoners. The humanitarian situation drew rare public comment from the Pope, who warned the world is growing indifferent to violence.

sources.title: Has the humanitarian crisis in Gaza been ignored?
Hamas armed wing says disarmament demands not acceptable
Israeli air strike hits crowd in Gaza City
Hamas armed wing rejects disarmament while Israel continues attacks
Rodent infestations and attacks spread in Gaza tent camps
‘Refuse to celebrate’: Christians in Gaza mark sombre Easter amid genocide
Pope warns world is becoming ‘indifferent’ to violence
Israel’s Ben-Gvir details plans for Palestinian ‘death row’

world.headline: Israel Strikes Lebanon as Easter Displaces Christian Families
world.body:
Israeli air strikes and shelling killed at least 14 people across Lebanon on Sunday, with attacks hitting multiple areas and pushing displacement figures higher. Christian families marked Easter far from their homes, unable to return to villages caught in the crossfire between Israeli forces and Hezbollah-linked targets.

Israel is now fighting on multiple fronts simultaneously - Gaza, Lebanon, and Iran - raising questions about the sustainability of the campaign and its toll on civilian populations across the region.

sources.title: Israel continues deadly attacks on Gaza while striking Iran, Lebanon
Israeli air strikes, shelling across Lebanon add to rising death toll
At least 14 people killed in Israeli strikes across Lebanon
Christian Lebanese families displaced as they celebrate Easter

world.headline: Pipeline Bomb Plot Uncovered; Migrant Boat Capsizes Near Libya
world.body:
Hungary and Serbia announced they had found explosives planted on a Russian natural gas pipeline ahead of Hungary's upcoming election. Budapest alleged a deliberate plot to sabotage the infrastructure, though no group has claimed responsibility. The discovery has heightened political tensions in both countries.

Separately, dozens of migrants went missing after their boat capsized in the Mediterranean near Libya. Search and rescue operations were launched, but conditions in the area complicated efforts. The incident is the latest in a long series of deadly crossings as migration pressure on southern European borders continues.

sources.title: Dozens missing after boat carrying migrants capsizes near Libya
Hungary alleges plot to blow up gas pipeline ahead of election
Serbia, Hungary say explosives found at Russian gas pipeline
Dozens missing after migrant boat capsizes in Mediterranean

ai.headline: Gemma 4 Arrives on iPhones and Local Machines
ai.body:
Google's Gemma 4 model is now running on consumer hardware, with an iOS app landing on the App Store and developers already pairing it with LM Studio's new headless CLI and Claude Code for local inference. The speed of adoption signals growing appetite for capable models that don't require a cloud connection.

Running frontier-class models locally matters because it cuts latency, cost, and privacy risk in one move. The fact that Gemma 4 fits on a phone marks a real threshold - not a demo, a daily-use tool.

sources.title: Gemma 4 on iPhone
Running Gemma 4 locally with LM Studio's new headless CLI and Claude Code

ai.headline: Microsoft Calls Copilot Entertainment, Not a Serious Tool
ai.body:
Microsoft's terms of use now describe Copilot as being "for entertainment purposes only" - a quiet legal disclaimer that contradicts the product's heavy marketing as a productivity assistant. The language gives Microsoft cover if the AI produces wrong or harmful output, but it also undermines every enterprise pitch the company has made.

This lands alongside a broader critique that Microsoft has lacked a coherent interface strategy for decades. Critics argue the company layers new products over unresolved foundations, and Copilot's disclaimer suggests the pattern continues.

sources.title: Copilot is ‘for entertainment purposes only,’ according to Microsoft’s terms of use
Microsoft Hasn't Had a Coherent GUI Strategy Since Petzold

ai.headline: Suno's Copyright Mess Exposes AI Music's Legal Fault Lines
ai.body:
Suno, the AI music generator, is generating covers and compositions that critics say sit in legally murky territory, raising questions about whether the company can survive sustained copyright scrutiny. The Verge's reporting highlights how the platform's outputs closely mirror protected works in ways that go beyond stylistic influence.

The music industry has been faster than most to litigate AI companies, and Suno's situation may become a test case. How courts rule will shape what AI audio tools can legally produce.

sources.title: Suno is a music copyright nightmare

ai.headline: Japan's Robot Workforce Fills Jobs, Not Headlines
ai.body:
Japan is deploying physical AI robots not to displace workers but to fill roles that go unfilled - care work, logistics, and manual labor in a country with a shrinking labor force. TechCrunch reports that several deployments are moving past pilot stage into genuine operational use, a milestone most Western robotics efforts haven't reached.

The framing matters. Where Western coverage obsesses over job displacement, Japan's demographic crisis makes robots a necessity rather than a threat. That context is producing more pragmatic, faster adoption.

sources.title: In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants

btc.headline: Bitcoin Holds at $67K as War Fears Crush Sentiment
btc.body:
Bitcoin is stuck near $67,000 with market sentiment at its worst levels since the Iran conflict began. Polymarket briefly ran markets on a US invasion of Iran, pushing odds to 63% before pulling them after intense backlash - a rare reversal for the prediction platform. The geopolitical noise is weighing hard on trader psychology, even as price holds relatively steady.

Some analysts argue the resilience is structural. Bitcoin ETFs have changed how the asset reacts to macro signals, with institutional flows now front-running Fed policy rather than responding to it. Still, at least one prominent trader calls new price lows a matter of time, citing weak momentum and a market that has failed to break higher despite months of opportunity.

sources.title: Polymarket odds of US invading Iran this year reach 63% after Trump's post
Polymarket pulls controversial Iran rescue markets after intense backlash
Bitcoin is now front-running the Fed rather than reacting to it. ETFs are the cause
Bitcoin holds steady as sentiment hits worst levels since Iran war began
New Bitcoin price lows 'matter of time' says trader with BTC stuck at $67K

btc.headline: North Korea Ran Six-Month Operation Behind $270M Drift Hack
btc.body:
The $270 million exploit of Solana-based exchange Drift was not a smash-and-grab. The platform says North Korean intelligence agents spent six months infiltrating its systems before executing the attack - a level of patience and sophistication that sets it apart from most crypto hacks.

The disclosure lands as Ledger's CTO warns that AI is actively making crypto security worse, lowering the cost and skill barrier for attackers. Together, the two stories paint a grim picture: threats are growing more advanced at the state level while becoming more accessible to ordinary criminals below it.

sources.title: AI is making crypto's security problem even worse, Ledger CTO warns
Drift says $270 million exploit was a six-month North Korean intelligence operation

btc.headline: Quantum Computing Threat to Bitcoin Is Real and Growing
btc.body:
A new explainer from CoinDesk lays out why quantum computing poses a genuine threat to Bitcoin's cryptographic foundations. The concern is not theoretical - advances in quantum hardware are accelerating, and the encryption protecting Bitcoin wallets was designed for a pre-quantum world.

The timing matters. Bitcoin is simultaneously being pitched as a long-term store of value and a complement to the US dollar, with a Bitcoin Policy Institute executive describing the two assets as having a symbiotic relationship. That narrative gets harder to sustain if the protocol's security model has a known expiration date that no one has yet agreed how to address.

sources.title: A simple explainer on what quantum computing actually is, and why it is terrifying for bitcoin
Bitcoin and the US dollar have a 'symbiotic' relationship: BPI exec

btc.headline: Token Oversupply and AI Agents Reshape Crypto's Infrastructure Debate
btc.body:
A Blockworks executive is calling token oversupply an existential problem for crypto, arguing that new supply is consistently outpacing any real value creation. The market is flooded with assets that have no durable reason to hold a price. It is a structural critique, not a cyclical one.

On the infrastructure side, Ant Group's blockchain arm has launched a platform letting AI agents transact directly on crypto rails - a concrete step toward machine-to-machine payments at scale. The contrast is sharp: the industry's token layer looks increasingly broken while its underlying rails are attracting serious builders with real-world backing.

sources.title: Ant Group’s blockchain arm unveils platform for AI agents to transact on crypto rails
‘Existential’ token problem seen as supply outpaces value creation: Blockworks exec

food.name: Miso-Glazed Aubergine (Nasu Dengaku)
food.description: Halved aubergines broiled until silky and caramelised under a sweet-savoury miso and mirin glaze
food.tip: Score the flesh in a crosshatch before glazing so the miso penetrates deeply rather than just sitting on the surface

exercise.name: The Sunday Tempo Ladder
exercise.description: A structured run alternating 3-minute tempo efforts at a comfortably hard pace with 90-second easy jogs, repeated six times, bookended by a 5-minute warm-up and cool-down walk

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity Over Time
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to the human body and mind, it becomes a genuinely unsettling question about whether the 'you' of ten years ago is the same person reading this now.
learn.why: Derek Parfit's modern take in 'Reasons and Persons' pushes this into neuroscience and ethics, touching on questions about memory, continuity, and why we fear death — an hour spent here tends to quietly rewire how you think about self-improvement, regret, and long-term planning
